title: Qatar

jokes.text: Sheikh Hamid offers condolences framing, Selina begins 'important work in the field of...' and aide completes it with 'Human trafficking' before she can say 'import-export'
jokes.setup: Selina is offering condolences and about to politely describe the deceased's work
jokes.punchline: The aide blurts 'Human trafficking' before Selina can sanitize it to 'import-export'
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep structure: the staff accidentally blurts the ugly truth the principal is trying to gloss over. The interruptive timing is precise and the contrast between the diplomatic register and 'human trafficking' is sharp. Economy is excellent — two words do enormous work.

jokes.text: It's like six degrees of Al-Qaeda in here. I hope we don't drone this place while we're in it.
jokes.setup: The delegation is navigating a room full of sketchy foreign dignitaries at a Qatari funeral reception
jokes.punchline: Selina's paranoid aside conflating the gathering with a terrorist network, topped by her fear of American drone strikes killing her own party
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'Al-Qaeda' to 'drone this place while we're in it' is classic Selina self-absorption — she takes a political observation and immediately makes it about her own survival. The drone line is the funnier of the two. Character-specific in that only a former president would frame drone strikes from the 'could I be the target?' angle.

jokes.text: He's dressed like a doorman.
jokes.setup: Aide warns Selina that Colonel Al-Saleh is a dangerous Sudanese warlord she cannot be photographed with
jokes.punchline: Selina's only objection is a shallow aesthetic observation about his uniform
jokes.explanation: Four words that perfectly encapsulate Selina's character: warned about a war criminal, she responds with a fashion critique. Exceptional character integration and economy. The joke IS the character — her vanity and obliviousness compressed to a single clause.

jokes.text: He just went full Chinaman on me.
jokes.setup: President Lu refuses to speak English to Selina despite clearly understanding her; she calls him out directly
jokes.punchline: Selina's obliviously offensive characterization of a diplomatic language snub
jokes.explanation: The joke is Selina's casual racism revealing her character, which is on-brand. The comedic concept (person uses antiquated/offensive term without realizing) isn't novel, but it's well-executed in context. Earns its place by characterizing Selina's diplomatic incompetence.

jokes.text: I have a dozen uncles. One less, more for the rest, right?
jokes.setup: Selina offers condolences on the death of Ambassador Al Jaffar to his nephew
jokes.punchline: The nephew treats his uncle's death with callous pragmatism, undermining the solemnity
jokes.explanation: Dark and efficient. The nephew's logic is airtight and horrifying — more inheritance logic than grief. Works as a subversion of the condolence scene and comments on the transactional nature of political funerals.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, well, tell it to that ingrate, the Dalai Lama. I guess he'll be thanking me in his next life.
jokes.setup: Selina sympathizes with the nephew about how Tibet played out; she's trying to seem magnanimous
jokes.punchline: Selina's bitterness toward the Dalai Lama breaks through, and she undercuts her own gesture with a sarcastic reincarnation joke
jokes.explanation: This joke is exceptional. 'I guess he'll be thanking me in his next life' uses Buddhist reincarnation doctrine as the vehicle for Selina's petty bitterness toward a Nobel Peace Prize winner. It's specific, perfectly structured, deeply character-integrated, and genuinely quotable. The pivot from sympathy performance to open resentment is characteristically Selina. Justifiably scores 8+.

jokes.text: Everyone say goat cheese!
jokes.setup: Aide clarifies the Pakistani ambassador wants a photo; Selina asks 'Shake?' meaning handshake — aide says better not
jokes.punchline: Instead of a normal photo cue, Selina blurts 'goat cheese' — culturally neutral (no pork product) but still absurdly provincial
jokes.explanation: Small but precise — Selina navigating Muslim diplomatic protocol by replacing 'cheese' with 'goat cheese' as if that's the fix. The specificity of the workaround is the joke. Her confidence in deploying it adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: that we're pregnant! — Oh, fuck! — Mom? — Oh, how could this happen?!
jokes.setup: Catherine excitedly announces pregnancy news to Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina's first reaction is 'Oh, fuck!' and 'Oh, how could this happen?!' — treating good news as a disaster
jokes.explanation: The timing of Selina's visceral 'Oh, fuck!' hitting exactly as Catherine says 'we're pregnant!' is a precision comedic cut. The escalation to 'Oh, how could this happen?!' is perfectly Selina — self-centered horror at the optics of becoming a grandmother. Structure is tight; the intercut makes it land harder.

jokes.text: Maybe I can stop masturbating now.
jokes.setup: Staff show Selina a 'Congratulations, Grandma!' poster about Catherine's pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Richard's non-sequitur response implying he masturbates in anticipation of being an uncle/grandfather figure, delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes. Richard's complete sincerity makes it work — he's not trying to be funny, he's genuinely processing the news through his own bizarre internal logic. The connection between the pregnancy news and his masturbation habits is never explained, which is exactly right. The 'I can't... no, no!' follow-up from someone in the room confirms the audience surrogate reaction. Richard's brand of innocent absurdism is maximally deployed here. Justifies 8+ on character integration and originality.

jokes.text: Omar Al-Saleh bombed his own people. He is a bomber and a photobomber!
jokes.setup: Selina is furious about the viral photo with the Sudanese warlord Colonel Al-Saleh
jokes.punchline: Selina invents a terrible pun connecting his war crimes to the photobomb
jokes.explanation: A genuinely funny pun that's made better by the fact that Selina is proud of it — she's treating a mass atrocity as a setup for wordplay, which is character comedy layered on top of the wordplay. 'Bomber and photobomber' is quotable precisely because it's simultaneously clever and appalling.

jokes.text: That is not true. You have amazing calves.
jokes.setup: Selina dramatically says her international reputation is 'literally the only thing I have left'
jokes.punchline: Gary earnestly tries to comfort her by complimenting her calves
jokes.explanation: Gary's comfort is so misaligned — she's lamenting the destruction of her legacy and he pivots to physical compliments — that it reveals the entire Gary-Selina codependency in six words. Maximum character integration; this joke could only come from Gary. The economy is perfect.

jokes.text: What is the opposite of a warlord? — A peace lady? — Yes, thank you very much, Richard!
jokes.setup: Selina asks rhetorically what image could counter the warlord photo
jokes.punchline: Richard answers with earnest literalism ('a peace lady') and Selina unironically validates it
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Richard's literal-minded answer to a rhetorical question, and Selina's genuine enthusiasm for a response that isn't actually helpful. Her 'Richard is thinking. He understands.' is even funnier — she's praising minimal competence as genius.

jokes.text: And why don't you have your phone right now? / I don't have international data, ma'am. / Mike! Please!
jokes.setup: Crisis mode; Selina expects her staff to be on top of things
jokes.punchline: Mike hasn't brought his phone because he didn't pay for international data — an absurdly cheap and unprofessional excuse from a senior aide
jokes.explanation: Mike's hapless incompetence is well-established, and this is a solid instance of it. The international data detail is specific and embarrassing. The running gag of Mike being cheap with his phone continues through the scene.

jokes.text: Human rights activist candidate search: no legs, acid-scarred face, already executed — followed by 'Full quota of limbs. She's got a whole face.'
jokes.setup: Staff try to find a female human rights hero for Selina to be photographed with to counter the warlord image
jokes.punchline: Each candidate is rejected for a physical disfigurement or death, escalating the horror until Nyaring is celebrated for simply having all her limbs and face intact
jokes.explanation: This escalating sequence is structurally excellent Veep comedy: each rejection is funnier than the last, with 'She was executed' topped only by 'unless... no, that's crazy.' The celebration of 'full quota of limbs / whole face' as positive attributes reveals how low the bar has been set, and Selina's shallow criteria are savagely exposed throughout.

jokes.text: I can't be with somebody who's physically disfigured. It makes me feel sick, okay?
jokes.setup: Amy proposes the acid-attack survivor as a candidate for the photo op
jokes.punchline: Selina's blunt, unvarnished rejection on the grounds of personal discomfort with disability
jokes.explanation: Selina's complete absence of self-awareness — rejecting a human rights icon because disfigurement makes her feel sick — is a distilled portrait of her character. The parenthetical 'okay?' at the end, as if seeking validation, sharpens the horror.

jokes.text: Damn it, I'm roaming. I can't afford to roam! / How do you take the battery out? / That doesn't come out.
jokes.setup: Staff find Nyaring as the perfect candidate; Mike is dealing with his phone throughout
jokes.punchline: Mike's ongoing battle with international phone charges continues as a counterpoint to the serious political discussion
jokes.explanation: The running gag of Mike's phone cheapness pays off here as an absurdist counterpoint — while Sudan is being arranged, Mike is panicking about a $3 roaming charge. Functional running gag that rewards attention.

jokes.text: Ugh, God! Well, that's not gonna be on camera, so.
jokes.setup: Staff explain Nyaring underwent female genital cutting as a child
jokes.punchline: Selina's only concern about this human rights abuse is whether it will be visible on camera
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character integration. Selina's 'not gonna be on camera, so' perfectly captures how she processes all human suffering through the lens of optics. The 'so' trailing off — as if this answers everything — is the key comedic beat. Could only be Selina.

jokes.text: One time, my mom brought in my breakfast an hour late like an idiot, so I just threw it on the floor... But it threw off my whole bathroom schedule and I ended up having to poop in a Sunoco bathroom.
jokes.setup: Jonah is asked to focus on issues that matter to working people; instead he launches into a personal story about Daylight Saving Time
jokes.punchline: A rambling anecdote about throwing food at his mother that ends with defecating in a gas station bathroom — presented as a political observation
jokes.explanation: Jonah's inability to relate politically or socially is exemplified here — asked about working Americans, he tells an anecdote about abusing his mother over breakfast scheduling that culminates in a gas station bathroom. The non-sequitur escalation is perfect Jonah, though slightly long. 'Sunoco bathroom' is a very specific, funny detail.

jokes.text: Libertonians. Says what we're about. — No, it sounds like a gay a cappella group.
jokes.setup: Jonah proposes 'Libertonians' as the name for their political caucus
jokes.punchline: Immediate deflation: it sounds like a gay a cappella group
jokes.explanation: Sharp and efficient deflation. 'Gay a cappella group' is specific enough to be genuinely funny rather than generic. The speed of the dismissal is key — no buildup needed.

jokes.text: I got it... the Beltway Boys. — Jesus Christ, are you tag-teaming this? Those are awful.
jokes.setup: The group continues naming their caucus; the next suggestion is 'the Beltway Boys'
jokes.punchline: Kent's exasperated 'are you tag-teaming this?' implies the suggestions are so bad they couldn't come from just one person
jokes.explanation: The 'tag-teaming' line is the best part — it implies coordination in stupidity. Solid supporting beat in the naming sequence.

jokes.text: Hamilton wasn't a president. — Then why the fuck did they write a musical about him?! — No, he was our first Puerto Rican president.
jokes.setup: Jonah suggests 'the Jeffersons' as their caucus name, referencing Jefferson the president
jokes.punchline: Jonah doesn't know Hamilton wasn't a president; when corrected, his colleague's explanation is that Hamilton was 'our first Puerto Rican president'
jokes.explanation: Two-layer joke of exceptional quality. First: Jonah doesn't know Hamilton was never president. Second: his colleague's correction — 'he was our first Puerto Rican president' — is a reference to Lin-Manuel Miranda's casting but delivered as historical fact, which is its own form of ignorance. The joke satirizes both political illiteracy and the Hamilton cultural phenomenon simultaneously. 'Then why the fuck did they write a musical about him?!' is perfectly Jonah. Justifies 8+ on every metric.

jokes.text: No, I am the white Hamilton of the Jeffersons, and that's our name.
jokes.setup: Group is debating the caucus name after the Hamilton confusion
jokes.punchline: Jonah crowns himself 'the white Hamilton of the Jeffersons' — a phrase that's internally contradictory and reveals he understood nothing from the conversation
jokes.explanation: 'The white Hamilton of the Jeffersons' is a deeply funny construction — Hamilton and Jefferson were political enemies, making this phrase incoherent; 'white Hamilton' references his erasure of Miranda's casting; and 'of the Jeffersons' references the Black TV show. Jonah synthesizes his own ignorance into a title that inadvertently illuminates all of it. Exceptionally quotable, exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: 'Nobody's gonna keep us down because we are moving on up.' / '♪ The Capitol Hills ♪'
jokes.setup: Jonah toasts 'the Jeffersons' and alludes to the TV theme ('moving on up')
jokes.punchline: Kent can't resist completing the joke with a sung 'The Capitol Hills' — a pun on the show 'The Jeffersons' ('The Capitol Hills' = 'The Fresh Prince'/'The Jeffersons') — before being told to shut up
jokes.explanation: The joke is partly Kent breaking character to do a bit — unexpected given his usual rigidity. The punning song reference is fun but not especially original. The 'God damn it, shut up' response is the funnier beat.

jokes.text: I call it Yoloha. It's a combination of YOLO and aloha. — Well, Buzzy, I think it's rad. — Which is a combination of retarded and sad.
jokes.setup: Dan shows off his self-designed tie with a portmanteau name
jokes.punchline: Buzzy responds that 'rad' is a compliment — then reveals it's his own portmanteau: 'retarded and sad'
jokes.explanation: Structurally near-perfect. The setup primes you to accept 'rad' as genuine praise, then the punchline reveals it's a portmanteau matching Dan's exact format, making it a meta-insult disguised as participation. The structure mirrors and weaponizes the exact joke form Dan introduced. Economy is exceptional — the whole thing is three lines. Justifies 8+ on structure and originality.

jokes.text: Okay, first of all, I never miss a glutes day.
jokes.setup: Dan is defending himself against the implication he's going soft for Jane
jokes.punchline: His defense begins with his gym schedule, not a denial of the relationship
jokes.explanation: Dan's vanity is his defining character trait, so defending himself with gym stats is perfectly in character. The non sequitur of leading with 'glutes day' rather than the relationship accusation is the joke.

jokes.text: Honestly, if you fucked in front of us, it'd be more dignified. — Ah, like gorillas at the zoo.
jokes.setup: Dan protests that he and Jane are not together
jokes.punchline: Buzzy says public sex would be more dignified than their current behavior; colleague adds 'like gorillas at the zoo'
jokes.explanation: The 'gorillas at the zoo' tag is a funnier, more specific image than it needs to be. The gorilla comparison — implying animalistic, public, undignified sex — elevates what could be a generic insult.

jokes.text: Jane just thinks the cold makes her and Brie's nipples pop more, and she's not wrong.
jokes.setup: Dan complains about the freezing studio temperature
jokes.punchline: The producer confirms this is a deliberate strategy for viewer engagement, delivered without embarrassment
jokes.explanation: 'And she's not wrong' is the key beat — the producer's matter-of-fact validation of this as a legitimate broadcast strategy satirizes morning TV's exploitation of female co-hosts. The absence of shame is what makes it dark.

jokes.text: Back in America, people are always telling me to go back to Africa. I'm actually glad I did, 'cause it's magical out here.
jokes.setup: The delegation is now in Sudan for the Nyaring photo op; Mike observes the scenery
jokes.punchline: Mike reframes a racist slur directed at him as good advice he's now grateful for
jokes.explanation: A clever subversion: Mike takes a racist attack and reframes it as accidental wisdom. The structure is a textbook misdirection — you expect him to be upset, instead he's grateful. The 'it's magical out here' innocence of the conclusion makes it land. Highly quotable and more layered than it first appears.

jokes.text: I know! It's sort of like a 'Vogue' shoot. — It really is.
jokes.setup: In the middle of Sudan's humanitarian crisis zone, a companion compliments Selina's Gucci scarf
jokes.punchline: Selina agrees the crisis visit looks like a fashion shoot, entirely without self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Selina's 'Vogue shoot' comparison in a poverty-stricken conflict zone is a sharp satirical beat about celebrity humanitarianism. Character-specific in Selina's performative empathy.

jokes.text: We are surrounded by land mines. You're just like Princess Di.
jokes.setup: A companion reveals they're in a land mine zone
jokes.punchline: Comparing Selina to Princess Diana — who famously worked in mine-affected areas and also died suddenly — as if it's a compliment
jokes.explanation: The Princess Di comparison lands because it's both complimentary (the mine activism) and terrifying (Diana's fate). The immediate subtext — Princess Di died in a crash while being chased by paparazzi, and they're in a mine field — makes this darker than it initially appears. The follow-up 'But with a better nose, of course' adds a vanity layer.

jokes.text: But with a better nose, of course. — Oh, stop it. — Okay, you know what? Why don't you just go ahead, though? — What? — Yep, good idea. You go ahead.
jokes.setup: They're standing in a land mine field; companion flatters Selina
jokes.punchline: Gary and Selina essentially redirect the companion to walk ahead of them through the mine field
jokes.explanation: The implication is clear and played completely straight: they're using the flattering companion as a human mine-detector. 'We'll be right behind you' is the tell. This is dark, specific, and perfectly Selina — self-preservation dressed up as politeness. High character integration.

jokes.text: Gary, be careful 'cause I've got a lot of valuable stuff in that bag!
jokes.setup: They're retreating through a mine field; Gary carries Selina's bag
jokes.punchline: Selina's concern is for her possessions, not for Gary's life
jokes.explanation: In a land mine field, Selina is worried about her bag, not the person carrying it. This is character comedy at its purest — her priorities laid bare in a moment of danger. 'I've got a lot of valuable stuff in that bag' is the defining Selina line.

jokes.text: I'm assuming you fly direct into Dayton? — Every other Friday.
jokes.setup: Someone is arguing for the importance of Dayton Municipal Airport to the nation's transportation system
jokes.punchline: The advocate casually admits they personally rely on that exact airport bi-weekly, revealing self-interest
jokes.explanation: A clean, efficient exposure of political self-interest. The 'every other Friday' specificity implies a life arranged around this convenience. Good economy; the joke is the admission.

jokes.text: What happens when you eat cheese, Will? — I go poo-poo in my panty-wanties.
jokes.setup: Furlong uses Will's lactose intolerance to humiliate him publicly
jokes.punchline: Will has been coached to say 'I go poo-poo in my panty-wanties' in a baby voice, with his pinky out
jokes.explanation: Furlong as a congressional abuser forcing his aide to perform his own humiliation for an audience is disturbing and hilarious. 'Poo-poo in my panty-wanties' is one of the most quotable lines in this episode — infantile, specific, and performed sincerely by Will. The 'with the pinky' choreography reveals how thoroughly Furlong has trained this performance. High character integration for both characters.

jokes.text: Furlong repeats the 'poo-poo in my panty-wanties' exercise WITH PINKY, then Will gives the identical answer
jokes.setup: Furlong wants Will to redo his cheese answer with a pinky out for refinement
jokes.punchline: Will gives the exact same answer regardless
jokes.explanation: The callback doubles the joke — the pinky instruction implies Furlong thinks Will's delivery wasn't refined enough, which is absurd. The identical answer suggests either Will is too broken to improvise or simply can't make those words more elegant. Escalates via repetition.

jokes.text: Put Wisconsin at the top of my shit list. — You want me to bump Sammy Hagar? — Make them 1A and 1B.
jokes.setup: Furlong is angry about missing the cheese curds from Wisconsin's delegation
jokes.punchline: He has a 'shit list' that already features Sammy Hagar, making Wisconsin compete with the Van Halen vocalist for his wrath
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Sammy Hagar being on a sitting congressman's active shit list is brilliantly absurd. That Wisconsin must share the top spot with a rock musician over missed cheese makes the trivial-political mix absurdly funny. Very Veep in its specificity.

jokes.text: Wait, so you need something from me? — Oh, no, it understands.
jokes.setup: Furlong has laid out his demand for Jonah's votes in exchange for the dinner invitation
jokes.punchline: Jonah slowly grasps the transactional nature of the deal; Furlong addresses him as 'it' — not 'he'
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, no, it understands' — Furlong dehumanizes Jonah with a pronoun switch mid-scene. The pause before Furlong's answer implies genuine surprise that the creature has worked it out. This is Furlong's brand of contempt at its most refined.

jokes.text: You are going to invite me to dinner at your house or I am going to fuck all your shit up. And you're gonna make me the paella.
jokes.setup: Furlong is negotiating with Jonah for votes on his airport bill
jokes.punchline: His leverage demand escalates from dinner to a specific dish: paella
jokes.explanation: Furlong's extortion pivoting from political leverage to a specific culinary demand is perfectly absurd. 'Fuck all your shit up' is the kind of congressional threat that shouldn't end with a paella request. The specificity of the dish — not 'make me dinner' but 'make me the paella' — elevates it.

jokes.text: Whoa, what's this, the fourth horseface of the Apocalypse? Jesus, Jonah, if you're gonna pay for sex, just add the extra two bucks for the premium edition.
jokes.setup: Jonah introduces Shawnee Tanz; Furlong reacts to seeing her
jokes.punchline: Furlong's double-barrel insult: calling her 'the fourth horseface of the Apocalypse' then implying she looks like discount prostitution
jokes.explanation: 'Fourth horseface of the Apocalypse' is an original riff on the Four Horsemen that's specific enough to be funny on its own. The 'extra two bucks' escalation adds a second layer of degradation. Both are pure Furlong in their casual cruelty. The jokes are mean but Furlong's consistent villainy makes them land as character comedy.

jokes.text: Ah, Ms. Tanz! Rumors of your beauty have not been exaggerated. — Yeah, he's bringing me to dinner. We're engaged. — Wait, what? We are?
jokes.setup: After insulting Shawnee behind her back, Furlong pivots to fulsome flattery when she's within earshot; Shawnee announces she and Jonah are engaged
jokes.punchline: Jonah didn't know he was engaged
jokes.explanation: Three-part structure: Furlong's sycophancy 180°, Shawnee's announcement, and Jonah's genuine surprise at his own engagement. The reveal that Jonah is being managed by the Tanz family like property is both funny and sad. Jonah's 'Wait, what? We are?' is perfectly calibrated — not played for broad comedy, just genuine confusion.

jokes.text: Will one of you please bathe and dress him? — I call dress. Sorry, Kent.
jokes.setup: Furlong invites Jonah to dinner but adds conditions
jokes.punchline: The staff are asked to prepare Jonah like a child; Kent loses the choice to Kent's colleague
jokes.explanation: The bathing and dressing demand treats Jonah like a toddler, which is consistent with Furlong's worldview. Kent's apologetic 'Sorry, Kent' as he's stuck with bathing duty is a good quick beat.

jokes.text: You look like an old dyke in an English gardening show.
jokes.setup: Mike appears in Sudan in some kind of outfit/hat appropriate for the environment
jokes.punchline: Selina's extremely specific insult comparing him to a gardening show archetype
jokes.explanation: Selina's insults are always more vivid when they're oddly specific. 'English gardening show' is a weirdly precise reference that gives the insult a visual dimension. It's offensive but the absurd specificity lifts it above generic mockery.

jokes.text: And so I give you this peace lily as a symbol of your brave stand for freedom. — It needs to be watered twice daily. — We have no water here.
jokes.setup: Selina presents Nyaring with a peace lily as a diplomatic gift
jokes.punchline: The plant requires water twice daily; Nyaring notes there is no water
jokes.explanation: This is sharp political satire delivered with perfect economy. The gift of a water-dependent plant to someone in a water-scarce crisis zone is an indictment of performative Western humanitarianism. Nyaring's deadpan 'We have no water here' is the most damning possible response. The beat earns its emotional resonance.

jokes.text: Selina gives Nyaring a peace lily. 'It needs to be watered twice daily.' / 'We have no water here.'
jokes.setup: Selina presents a peace lily as a symbol of freedom to a woman living under house arrest in drought-stricken Sudan
jokes.punchline: Nyaring flatly notes they have no water to maintain it
jokes.explanation: A perfect metaphor made literal: Selina's goodwill gesture is literally impossible to sustain in its environment. The peace lily needs water twice daily in a place without water — exactly like Selina's peace efforts need substance they don't have. Whether intentional or not, it's brilliant. Nyaring's flat delivery seals it. Selina's pivot to 'we can just put it with the recycling' makes it worse.

jokes.text: Oh. Oh, well, that's fine. We can just put it with the recycling. — That is my bed.
jokes.setup: Selina dismisses the plant problem by suggesting recycling
jokes.punchline: What Selina identifies as a recycling area is actually Nyaring's bed
jokes.explanation: Four words — 'That is my bed' — constitute a devastating satirical punchline. Selina's casual misidentification of a human's sleeping space as a recycling bin is the clearest illustration of the gulf between her world and Nyaring's. The economy is exceptional. Nyaring's deadpan delivery amplifies it. This is among the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: Wow, it... wow, that's a cozy bed.
jokes.setup: Nyaring corrects Selina that the 'recycling' is her bed
jokes.punchline: Selina's recovery: 'that's a cozy bed' — a transparently hollow observation
jokes.explanation: Selina's pivot to 'cozy bed' is the cringe recovery that makes the previous joke land even harder. The false cheerfulness and the 'wow...wow' stutter reveal how badly she's scrambling. A perfect cringe-comedy recovery beat.

jokes.text: Come, let me serve my American visitors tea while over half the Sudanese suffer in starvation.
jokes.setup: Nyaring offers hospitality to her American visitors
jokes.punchline: She contextualizes her hospitality within the mass starvation happening outside simultaneously, making the tea offer absurd and accusatory
jokes.explanation: Nyaring serves tea while narrating humanitarian catastrophe in the same breath — the juxtaposition is devastating and funny. The joke is on the entire scenario of American politicians visiting crisis zones for photo ops. 'She's kind of a bitch' from Selina's aide is the perfect coda. Justifies a high score for satirical precision.

jokes.text: She's kind of a bitch.
jokes.setup: Nyaring has offered tea while listing the suffering of her people
jokes.punchline: An aide's whispered assessment of Nyaring as 'kind of a bitch'
jokes.explanation: The aide's whispered assessment of the human rights dissident as a bitch — for telling the truth about her situation — is the satirical gut-punch. Four words that indict the entire American political visitor mindset. Economy of 9 is deserved.

jokes.text: No, it was a purposeful desecration of the man who beat and raped me. — Well, they go with everything.
jokes.setup: Selina calls the death-shroud curtains 'a touching tribute'; Nyaring corrects this
jokes.punchline: Nyaring reveals the curtains are a deliberate desecration of her abuser; Selina's response is an interior design observation
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single best joke. Nyaring's correction — the curtains are a desecration of her rapist — should stop the conversation cold. Selina's 'Well, they go with everything' treats the revelation as a color palette observation. The structure is perfect: setup with misidentification, first correction (death shroud), second correction (rapist), and then a punchline that is completely disconnected from human reality. The economy is maximal. Nyaring's story is genuinely horrifying; Selina's response is genuinely horrifying in a different register. This justifies the highest scores in the episode.

jokes.text: It sends a 'Po-ent' message... — Potent.
jokes.setup: Selina is describing why the photo op matters
jokes.punchline: She mispronounces 'potent' as 'po-ent'; Gary gently corrects her
jokes.explanation: A small malapropism beat that characterizes Selina's intellectual limitations without being mean-spirited. The gentle Gary correction is character-consistent. Functional but not standout.

jokes.text: You know that little animal that Nathan Lane plays in 'The Lion King'? — Oh, I love that guy! — Yes, yes. Uh, so, three of those got caught in the engine.
jokes.setup: There's a problem with the plane; the aide needs to explain what happened
jokes.punchline: Rather than say 'meerkat' or 'warthog,' the aide identifies the animals by Nathan Lane's character, and Selina's response is enthusiasm for Nathan Lane rather than alarm
jokes.explanation: Two layers: the aide's roundabout way of explaining a mechanical failure, and Selina's 'Oh, I love that guy!' response to hearing Nathan Lane's name rather than processing that animals are in the engine. Both character reactions are simultaneously plausible and absurd.

jokes.text: A beautiful woman lost in the desert with only a small group of complete incompetents to assist her? Yeah, they really are incompetent, aren't they? I've never seen anything quite like it, frankly. How do you do it?
jokes.setup: Jaffar appears to rescue Selina with his plane; he compliments her
jokes.punchline: Jaffar's 'compliment' about her incompetent staff leads Selina to... agree enthusiastically about how incompetent her staff are
jokes.explanation: Selina gleefully agrees that her staff are 'complete incompetents' and even asks 'How do you do it?' as if tolerating them is a burden rather than a reflection on her leadership. Pure character comedy.

jokes.text: I bet you say that to all your Western heretics.
jokes.setup: Jaffar tells Selina she doesn't have to leave so soon
jokes.punchline: Selina flirts back by riffing on the term 'Western heretics' — substituting a political/religious term for the expected 'Western girls'
jokes.explanation: A smart flirtatious line that works on political and romantic registers simultaneously. 'Heretics' is the unexpected word — funnier than 'girls' or 'women' and more self-aware about the cross-cultural dynamic they're navigating.

jokes.text: Dan is called into a meeting with HR, accused of sexually harassing Jane — turns out he had filed the complaint against Jane, not vice versa
jokes.setup: Dan is called into what appears to be a sexual harassment investigation — the audience assumes it's about his behavior toward women
jokes.punchline: The HR rep reveals it's Dan who filed a complaint that Jane harassed him
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is well-constructed. Dan's 'that wardrobe girl is crazy' is a deliberate red herring. The reveal that Dan is the victim and Jane is the perpetrator inverts the expected dynamic in a way that's structurally satisfying and thematically coherent with Jane's character.

jokes.text: You microdicked weasel, you couldn't even fill my pisshole.
jokes.setup: Jane learns Dan has filed an HR complaint against her for sexual harassment
jokes.punchline: Her response is a devastating anatomical insult that simultaneously confirms the harassment complaint
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's best lines. The insult is already shocking; 'pisshole' is specific and grotesque in a way 'you know' isn't. But the meta-joke is that Jane, in defending herself from harassment allegations, is actively committing harassment in front of the HR rep. The structural irony is perfect. Exceptional character integration — only Jane talks like this. Quotable, shocking, perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Let me advise you that I built this goddamn network with my own two tits!
jokes.setup: The HR rep tries to establish the meeting as a safe space
jokes.punchline: Jane's counterargument is that she built CBS with her breasts — asserting both her power and her awareness of how she achieved it
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. 'I built this goddamn network with my own two tits' is simultaneously a feminist declaration and a self-indictment — Jane is claiming power through the exact mechanism being criticized, and she knows it. The joke works on satirical, character, and shock comedy levels at once. Only Jane McCabe could say this and have it land. Justifies 8+ scores across the board.

jokes.text: Send the Egyptian president's mistress a gift. Any department store that wasn't started by Jews. I will have to start one myself.
jokes.setup: Selina issues orders while preparing for next steps in Sudan deal
jokes.punchline: Her anti-Semitic instruction followed by a literal aside that she'd have to start a non-Jewish department store
jokes.explanation: The casual anti-Semitism as a diplomatic consideration, followed by the practical impossibility of her criteria (I will have to start one myself), reveals how Selina's bigotry is both reflexive and self-defeating. The joke works as political satire through character.

jokes.text: What's Qatari for 'morning after pill'? — It's probably a stoning, which would also do the trick.
jokes.setup: Selina asks about emergency contraception in Qatar after her night with Jaffar
jokes.punchline: The aide notes that a stoning would technically achieve the same result
jokes.explanation: Dark and structurally clean. The aide's logic is horrifyingly correct — a stoning for extramarital sex would prevent a pregnancy in the most brutal way. The observation is made matter-of-factly, which is the Veep house style. Genuinely funny and genuinely uncomfortable.

jokes.text: You look like the business end of a baboon.
jokes.setup: Mike appears badly sunburned
jokes.punchline: Selina's colorful comparison of his face to baboon anatomy
jokes.explanation: 'The business end of a baboon' is a vivid, specific image — and a baboon's hindquarters are famously red and inflamed, which maps exactly onto Mike's sunburn. The specificity elevates it above generic insult.

jokes.text: You know, when I talk, I can hear my skin crunch. — Oh... Lord. — Makes me wanna eat cereal.
jokes.setup: Mike describes the sensation of his extreme sunburn
jokes.punchline: The crunching skin sound makes him want to eat cereal — a completely unhinged association
jokes.explanation: This is original and strange in exactly the right way. Mike's sensory logic — crunching skin sounds like cereal — is both disgusting and completely coherent within its own weird framework. The 'Oh... Lord' reaction from Selina is the perfectly calibrated response. Justifies 8+ for originality and economy.

jokes.text: Ben says Qataris are very good at inserting themselves. — You got that right. — Be as red. Be as red.
jokes.setup: Selina is commenting on Qatari political instinct ('inserting themselves'); Mike is sunburned and keeps repeating a phrase
jokes.punchline: The double entendre of 'inserting themselves' lands after her night with Jaffar; Mike's sunburned delirium ('be as red') continues as comic counterpoint
jokes.explanation: The 'inserting themselves' double entendre plays on the political and physical sense given Selina and Jaffar's liaison. Mike's recurring sunburn delirium ('be as red') is a running gag that adds texture without demanding full attention.

jokes.text: Now, Amy, for the first time in a long time, I feel like myself. Or, you know, at least the person I'm supposed to feel like.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to defend her romantic entanglement with Jaffar to Amy
jokes.punchline: Her self-correction — 'or at least the person I'm supposed to feel like' — undermines her confidence in her own identity
jokes.explanation: A rare moment of genuine pathos with a comedy edge. Selina's self-correction ('the person I'm supposed to feel like') reveals her fundamental uncertainty about who she is outside of power. It's funny because it's such an honest, deflating qualification, and it's sad for the same reason.

jokes.text: my political career had answered a craigslist ad for a modeling shoot in the Angeles National Forest.
jokes.setup: Amy warns that dating a Muslim man would be political suicide
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses this with an extended metaphor: her political career is so dead it's already wandered into a murder scenario (Angeles National Forest = where bodies are found)
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The Craigslist modeling shoot in the Angeles National Forest is a reference to a specific type of predatory scam that lures victims — meaning Selina's political career has already accepted its own murder invitation. It's dark, original, highly specific, and perfectly character-integrated. The metaphor is both funny and genuinely bleak. Justifies the highest craft scores in the episode.

jokes.text: Look, even their blinker is on. — Somebody's been using their Rosetta Stone CDs.
jokes.setup: The Chinese delegation's helicopter arrives
jokes.punchline: First, a visual joke about a helicopter using a blinker (absurdly rule-following); second, Selina mocking Lu for greeting her in English when she noted he ignored her earlier
jokes.explanation: Two quick jokes in succession. The blinker on a helicopter is a visual absurdism. The Rosetta Stone callback to Lu's earlier language snub is a satisfying payoff — now he's speaking English because he needs something from her.

jokes.text: No, you gave Montez Tibet. You gave me yak shit.
jokes.setup: Lu claims China gave Selina Tibet
jokes.punchline: Selina distinguishes between the real deal (Montez got Tibet) and the worthless version she received (yak shit)
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and character integration. 'You gave me yak shit' takes Tibet — a serious geopolitical issue — and summarizes Selina's slight in a single perfectly calibrated image. Yak shit is both geographically specific (Tibet/yaks) and perfectly expressive of worthlessness. This is efficient, original, and only Selina.

jokes.text: But unless you're planning on knitting your semiconductors out of bamboo shoots and panda cock, you're gonna need every ounce of gallium you can get in Sudan.
jokes.setup: Lu claims there are other sources for Sudan's minerals
jokes.punchline: Selina's rebuttal combines tech industry needs with absurdist Chinese-stereotyped materials
jokes.explanation: 'Knitting semiconductors out of bamboo shoots and panda cock' is one of the episode's most original and memorable lines. The combination of precision tech terminology with absurd Chinese-associated natural products creates a unique comedic image. The escalation from bamboo to 'panda cock' is perfectly structured. Selina's negotiating style — blending genuine intelligence with offensive reductiveness — is perfectly captured. Justifies 8+ on originality and character integration.

jokes.text: Rots of ruck with that.
jokes.setup: Lu claims there are other mineral sources
jokes.punchline: Selina responds with a racist mock-accent approximation of 'lots of luck'
jokes.explanation: Below-average by the episode's standards. The joke relies solely on a lazy racist accent approximation with no structural cleverness. Compared to the nuance of other Selina insults in this episode, this one is cheap. It gets a pass from complete failure because Selina's obliviousness is the frame, but the comedic concept itself is not original or well-crafted.

jokes.text: what's gonna happen when your honorable citizens can't put their kung pao chicken on the table and start to question this communist-capitalist shit swirly you've been serving up?
jokes.setup: Selina is pressing China to take the Sudan deal
jokes.punchline: 'Communist-capitalist shit swirly' as a description of Chinese economic policy
jokes.explanation: 'Communist-capitalist shit swirly' is a genuinely original and accurate characterization of China's economic paradox, expressed in juvenile terms. It's funny because it's both crude and intellectually correct. 'Shit swirly' as a political science term is the kind of Selina construction that's dumb-smart in the most entertaining way.

jokes.text: 'What's gonna happen when your honorable citizens can't put their kung pao chicken on the table and start to question this communist-capitalist shit swirly you've been serving up?'
jokes.setup: Selina is pressing Lu on China's economic vulnerability
jokes.punchline: 'Shit swirly' as a term for the Chinese economic model — communist-capitalist hybrid described as a toilet metaphor
jokes.explanation: A genuinely smart insult. 'Communist-capitalist shit swirly' is both politically accurate (China's hybrid model is genuinely contradictory) and vividly disgusting. The 'kung pao chicken' setup is a bit lazy in its Chinese stereotype, but 'shit swirly' as a economic policy descriptor is brilliant. Selina's negotiating style — escalating to schoolyard language while making valid geopolitical points — is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: Yu must really crave public acknowledgment. — Oh, yeah, and you must really crave a 6.7 GDP growth.
jokes.setup: Lu tries to psychoanalyze Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina counters with a macroeconomic jab — suggesting Lu is equally driven by GDP metrics
jokes.explanation: A clean comeback that flips the psychological frame into economic terms. Lu's 'Yu' (name pun intended or not) versus Selina's '6.7 GDP growth' shows she's done her homework. The parallel structure of the exchange is tight.

jokes.text: 'Confucius say fuck, yeah!' — Selina after closing the Sudan mineral deal with China.
jokes.setup: The deal closes successfully
jokes.punchline: Selina celebrates with a bastardized 'Confucius say' joke — replacing wisdom with profane celebration, revealing her culturally reductive excitement
jokes.explanation: Economical and in character — Selina immediately reaches for a Confucius joke to celebrate a deal with China, revealing her cultural shallow-end. The subversion of the fortune-cookie format with 'fuck, yeah!' is fun. Not original, but well-deployed.

jokes.text: Confucius say fuck, yeah!
jokes.setup: The deal is closed with China
jokes.punchline: Selina celebrates by invoking Confucius for a profane exclamation
jokes.explanation: The Confucius aphorism format applied to a profane celebration is efficient and funny. It's Selina at her most self-congratulatory and culturally oblivious. Four words, perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: I don't want this whole evening to go all Natalie Wood on me.
jokes.setup: Selina is celebrating on Jaffar's yacht and asks for a small amount of something
jokes.punchline: She references Natalie Wood — who drowned falling off a yacht — as a model of evening-ending catastrophe
jokes.explanation: A specific and dark cultural reference that works precisely because it's unexpected. They're on a yacht, which makes the Natalie Wood drowning reference both logical and terrifying. The casual delivery — 'go all Natalie Wood on me' — is perfect.

jokes.text: Mohammad H. Christ, that's my dissident.
jokes.setup: News breaks that President Montez has flown to Sudan and is presenting Nyaring with flowers
jokes.punchline: Selina's profanity uses 'Mohammad H. Christ' — combining Islamic and Christian exclamations — to express her fury
jokes.explanation: 'Mohammad H. Christ' is an original construction that blends religious blasphemies in a way that's appropriate for the Middle Eastern context and still recognizably Selina's character. The H. is the key detail — lifting it from 'Jesus H. Christ' and applying it to Muhammad.

jokes.text: I'm going back to motherfucking Sudan. — But you were just there. Aren't you having a good time? — Absolutely, and I'm gonna say what Titty Gonzalez was too chicken-shit to say.
jokes.setup: Selina's Sudan photo op was scooped by President Montez
jokes.punchline: Selina calls President Montez 'Titty Gonzalez' — a slur referencing both her appearance and ethnicity
jokes.explanation: Selina's nickname for Montez is consistent with her character's petty rivalry and casually offensive style. 'Titty Gonzalez' is both crude and specific enough to land. Earned-vs-cheap suffers slightly because the bigotry is the punchline rather than a layer beneath it.

jokes.text: I wanna say something about my paella. Now, it is so good, when you taste it, what's gonna happen, Will? — You're gonna wanna take it to a church and marry it, Rog.
jokes.setup: Furlong introduces a ritual about his paella at the dinner party
jokes.punchline: Will delivers the coached compliment with formalized sincerity — 'take it to a church and marry it'
jokes.explanation: A callback to Furlong's earlier performance with Will — now applied to paella instead of cheese humiliation. Will's coached compliment ('take it to a church and marry it') is earnest enough to be funny. The ritual nature of Furlong training Will for food compliments is a consistent character gag.

jokes.text: Damn straight! — Roger, that's a dollar in the swear jar. — Aw, crud. — That's $2! — What? — Potty mouth, potty mouth, Roger.
jokes.setup: Furlong's wife Julie runs a swear jar at the dinner party
jokes.punchline: The man who just told an aide he'd 'gouge out your eye and fuck your skull' is being charged a dollar for 'damn straight'
jokes.explanation: The contrast between Furlong's violence at the office and his domestic submission at home is the episode's funniest character reveal. 'Potty mouth, potty mouth, Roger' — Julie condescending to the congressman who threatens eye-socket violations — is an excellent character-integration joke.

jokes.text: Ah, Julie points out that to be a true Valenciana, one must add live snails just as the water comes to a boil.
jokes.setup: They're talking about the paella at dinner
jokes.punchline: Furlong relays his wife's culinary correction with complete deference, in stark contrast to his earlier 'I'll gouge your eye out' energy
jokes.explanation: Furlong's domestic deference continues as a running gag. The culinary pedantry is a nice touch, and the image of Furlong submissively conveying his wife's paella instructions is good character comedy.

jokes.text: And I want Rihanna to put a gun to my head while she makes me eat her out, but guess what, that's about as likely to happen as Will's wife putting a baby in her polyp festival of a uterus!
jokes.setup: In private with Jonah, Furlong drops the domestic performance and unleashes his real personality
jokes.punchline: His fantasy about Rihanna is used as a comparator for the impossibility of Will's wife conceiving — 'polyp festival of a uterus'
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's best jokes. 'Polyp festival of a uterus' is a spectacular coinage — medically specific, grotesque, and delivered with Furlong's characteristic escalation. The structure uses Rihanna as the absurd comparator that makes Will's wife situation feel equally impossible. The transition from swear-jar compliance to this line in the cellar is perfect character comedy. Justifies 8+ scores.

jokes.text: I would sooner gouge out your eye and fuck your skull from here to the end of time. — That's what we're promised, that Jesus is our Lord. — Hallelujah. Amen, Dad.
jokes.setup: Furlong is threatening Jonah in the wine cellar; the door opens and Julie hears only '...from here to the end of time'
jokes.punchline: Julie interprets this as religious sentiment; the family affirms it
jokes.explanation: Structurally exceptional. The eye-fucking threat ends with a phrase ('to the end of time') that maps perfectly onto Christian eschatology. Julie's interpretation as a Jesus declaration, met with 'Hallelujah. Amen, Dad.' from guests who only heard the ending, is a masterclass in comedic timing and misdirection. Furlong's violent private persona and compliant public one collide perfectly. Justifies the highest scores — comparable to the 8.5 anchor in structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Say, Jonah, how about I start looking for that new office for you first thing tomorrow?
jokes.setup: Jonah has successfully leveraged his vote for an office upgrade through the dinner situation
jokes.punchline: Furlong's sudden gracious offer — immediately after being blackmailed — delivered with charming dinner-party affect
jokes.explanation: Furlong's performative graciousness in extracting himself from blackmail is a good character beat. The contrast with his wine cellar behavior seconds earlier is effective. Functional rather than standout.

jokes.text: You look like the world's least-fucked geisha.
jokes.setup: Mike has applied zinc oxide (actually cocoa butter) as sunscreen and appears bizarrely made-up
jokes.punchline: Selina's image of a geisha who has been denied sex — 'least-fucked' — as applied to a sunburned white man
jokes.explanation: 'The world's least-fucked geisha' is a vivid, original image. The combination of the cultural reference (geisha), the unexpected modifier ('least-fucked'), and the visual of a sunburned white man's white-coated face creates a genuinely funny picture. Original and highly quotable.

jokes.text: She has such wonderful hair. — Oh, luxurious.
jokes.setup: Selina is discussing President Montez stealing her dissident photo op
jokes.punchline: Instead of political analysis, Selina and Gary discuss Montez's hair
jokes.explanation: A good character moment — Selina and Gary's preoccupation with appearance even amid political crisis. Gary's 'luxurious' is perfect sycophant agreement. Smaller beat but consistent with their dynamic.

jokes.text: Nyaring, I have come to do what President Montez did not do. — I will speak on behalf of our sisters, Nyaring.
jokes.setup: Selina arrives back in Sudan with an empowerment speech
jokes.punchline: The setup for her speech — which we then watch her completely betray — makes the declaration darkly ironic in retrospect
jokes.explanation: Works as setup for the speech betrayal payoff — earns rewatch value because knowing the speech is coming makes this declaration funnier in retrospect.

jokes.text: What happened to the little girl who served us tea? — Oh, she was married to the village elder. — Ah. — Saturday. — Well, please send along my congratulations.
jokes.setup: Selina asks about the young girl from her first visit
jokes.punchline: The girl has been married off to the village elder (i.e., forced child marriage); Selina's response is to send congratulations
jokes.explanation: Devastating satirical structure. 'Saturday' is the perfect beat — the specificity of when the child marriage occurred, delivered casually, makes it more horrifying. Selina's 'please send along my congratulations' is a perfect satirical capsule of Western diplomatic complicity — she doesn't process what she's been told because processing it would be inconvenient. Economy is exceptional. Justifies 8+ across the board.

jokes.text: It's an Arab conference on human rights. You can pretty much have any slot you want. — Yeah. — After the men, of course. — Right.
jokes.setup: Selina asks how she got such a good speaking slot last-minute
jokes.punchline: Arab conference on human rights — slots are freely available; then the caveat that women speak after men reveals the conference's fundamental contradiction
jokes.explanation: A two-part satirical strike. First: slots at an Arab human rights conference are freely available (nobody wants to speak because it's a sham). Second: the immediate qualifier — 'after the men, of course' — exposes the conference as antithetical to its stated purpose. The structure is impeccable and the economy is near-perfect. Selina's resigned 'Right' is the perfect reaction.

jokes.text: What happened? — Turns out it wasn't zinc oxide. I was wearing cocoa butter. — You look like redheaded bacon.
jokes.setup: Mike's sunscreen situation has escalated — he used cocoa butter instead of zinc oxide
jokes.punchline: Selina compares the result to 'redheaded bacon'
jokes.explanation: Third in the Mike sunburn insult escalation. 'Redheaded bacon' is a vivid and original image — the cocoa butter has both darkened and crisped the sunburn. Each insult in the series is distinct and increasingly specific. Good escalation structure.

jokes.text: NuvaRing is gonna dig this a lot. — It's Nyaring.
jokes.setup: Staff are discussing how the speech will play for Nyaring
jokes.punchline: An aide calls her 'NuvaRing' — the contraceptive device — which Selina corrects
jokes.explanation: The mispronunciation of a human rights activist's name as a contraceptive brand is an inadvertently apt joke given the FGM themes. Selina's correction implies she's actually learned the name, which is character development of the smallest kind.

jokes.text: Ma'am, I'm just so optimistic about the world we're creating for your coming grandchild. — Wait, what? Catherine's pregnant? — Ma'am, do you prefer Gram-Gram or Mee-Maw?
jokes.setup: Richard tries to be encouraging about the speech
jokes.punchline: Selina has apparently forgotten Catherine's pregnancy announcement from the beginning of the episode; Richard offers grandmother name options before Selina can process the reminder
jokes.explanation: Richard's pivot to 'Gram-Gram or Mee-Maw' before Selina can process the news is perfect Richard timing. The callback to Catherine's pregnancy, revealed that Selina has completely forgotten in the chaos of the episode, is both a character joke and a structural one. Selina's 'What did you just say?' doubles as both reactions.

jokes.text: Did you see those paparazzi shots of us in the 'Daily Mail'? — I did. — Those scum-sucking vultures got some really good shots of us.
jokes.setup: Jaffar mentions paparazzi photos from their time together
jokes.punchline: He calls the photographers 'scum-sucking vultures' but then praises the quality of their shots
jokes.explanation: The pivot from insult to compliment — 'scum-sucking vultures' who 'got some really good shots' — is a nice character moment. Jaffar's vanity (caring how he looks in the photos) is briefly exposed.

jokes.text: Selina's speech where she pivots from women's rights to defending female genital mutilation with 'who are we to judge?' and 'glass houses' — ending with 'And female circumcision... I'm sure you have your reasons.'
jokes.setup: Selina was supposed to deliver a strong women's rights speech; she's been told not to embarrass Al-Saleh to protect the deal
jokes.punchline: Her speech devolves from 'women's rights are human rights' to essentially endorsing FGM on the grounds of cultural relativism and male circumcision equivalence
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic centerpiece and a contender for the best extended cringe sequence in the run. Every beat escalates the horror: starting with 'women's rights are human rights' — borrowed from Hillary Clinton — then systematically dismantling it. 'Who are we to judge?' applied to FGM is the satirical gut-punch. 'Glass houses' as a reference to circumcision is both logically absurd and politically cowardly in a way that's perfectly Selina. 'I'm sure you have your reasons' is the devastating final note. This sequence has been earned across the entire episode — every scene of Selina choosing optics over principle leads here. Highest craft scores in the episode are justified.

jokes.text: I can legislate the shit out of shit in here.
jokes.setup: Jonah surveys his new office (same as old office, three floors lower)
jokes.punchline: His proclamation of legislative potency in a demonstrably worse office
jokes.explanation: 'Legislate the shit out of shit' is a good Jonah construction — profane, tautological, and grandiose about a meaningless achievement. Character-consistent.

jokes.text: Could you tell the Colossus of Chodes that this is the exact same goddamn office, only three floors down with a shittier view?
jokes.setup: Jonah is thrilled about his new office
jokes.punchline: Furlong's aside naming Jonah 'the Colossus of Chodes' while noting the office is objectively worse
jokes.explanation: 'Colossus of Chodes' is an excellent Furlong epithet — combining grandiose classical reference with anatomical vulgarity, which is Furlong's signature mode. The observation that the office is worse validates the audience's suspicion.

jokes.text: Second goddamn floor, I can't even commit suicide. — I got a key to the roof. We could do a Butch-Sundance. — Nah, I'll just wait for cancer.
jokes.setup: Kent reflects on the new office's inadequacy
jokes.punchline: Suicide via the second floor is impossible; the roof is offered as an alternative; Kent declines in favor of passive cancer
jokes.explanation: Three-beat dark comedy sequence. 'Can't even commit suicide' from the second floor is the absurd setup; the Butch and Sundance reference (jumping to certain death) is a cultured escalation; 'I'll just wait for cancer' is the perfect nihilistic landing. Kent's character here is unexpectedly developed — the most resigned man in Washington. Justifies 8+ on structure and economy.

jokes.text: President Lu says, 'Thank you for all your nothing.'
jokes.setup: Selina greets Lu warmly; he appears to respond
jokes.punchline: The translator reveals Lu's actual message: 'Thank you for all your nothing'
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. Lu's 'Thank you for all your nothing' is a devastating diplomatic dismissal — a formal courtesy that contains total contempt. The translation framing (Lu has returned to not speaking English directly) brings the Lu language game full circle. 'Thank you for all your nothing' is among the episode's most quotable lines.

jokes.text: Who went pee-pee in his Coke?
jokes.setup: The deal has collapsed; Selina reacts to Lu's dismissal
jokes.punchline: Selina frames the diplomatic failure as a juvenile prank
jokes.explanation: Selina's infantile metaphor for diplomatic collapse — 'who went pee-pee in his Coke' — is an effective character beat. It reveals how she processes complex geopolitical failure: as a schoolyard dispute.

jokes.text: What do I have to do, pull out my clit and cut it off in front of everyone to get anything done in this world?
jokes.setup: The Sudan deal has collapsed because Selina's speech wasn't supportive enough of Al-Saleh
jokes.punchline: Selina's exasperated rhetorical question using FGM as her own hypothetical performance of commitment
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines and a near-perfect structural callback. After an episode of cowardly handling of FGM — rejecting activists because of their appearance, defending it in a speech — Selina's rage finally inverts the trope: she'd perform the act herself to get the deal. The shock value is earned by the entire episode's setup. It captures Selina's desperation, her obliviousness to the irony, and her transactional view of all human concerns. Justifies 8+ comprehensively.

jokes.text: You know, white. — Wow. Okay, that is racist. — Is it?
jokes.setup: Jaffar ends their relationship, saying he can't be with 'someone like you'
jokes.punchline: He means white — race is his objection, not age or anything Selina feared; his 'Is it?' response to being called racist
jokes.explanation: The joke works on the subversion of Selina's expectation (she feared age-shaming) and the inversion of the typical racism dynamic — Selina, usually the offender, is on the receiving end and correctly identifies it. Jaffar's 'Is it?' is delightfully ambiguous — genuine confusion or provocation. Smart structural reversal.

jokes.text: 'So, what's next, huh? Liberian refugee crisis might be sexy.'
jokes.setup: Jaffar is consoling Selina about the deal collapsing
jokes.punchline: He describes humanitarian crises in terms of their 'sexiness' as PR opportunities
jokes.explanation: 'Liberian refugee crisis might be sexy' is a good dark joke — treating refugee crises as PR product in the marketplace of sympathetic causes. Jaffar's consumerist framing of humanitarian disasters as 'sexy' is perfectly cynical and characterizes him well.

jokes.text: I thought that we would be traveling around the world on your yacht, solving international crises and... — We will always have the yacht. — But you just said we won't. — I meant the memory of the yacht.
jokes.setup: Selina imagines their future together; Jaffar breaks up with her
jokes.punchline: 'We will always have the yacht' immediately undercut by Selina's correct observation that he just said they won't; Jaffar pivots to 'the memory of the yacht' — a Casablanca parody
jokes.explanation: 'We will always have the yacht' is a Casablanca riff ('We'll always have Paris') that Selina immediately deflates with correct logic. Jaffar's recovery — 'the memory of the yacht' — is both a dodge and funnier than the original. The exchange is quick and well-structured.

jokes.text: But your dad wasn't pleased because he thought I was white, right? I mean, not because he thought there was any age difference between us, 'cause there practically isn't one. — Oh, yeah, yeah, it was just the white thing. — He also thought you might be Jewish. — Well, I'm not.
jokes.setup: Selina needs confirmation that the breakup is about race, not age
jokes.punchline: Jaffar adds that his father also thought she was Jewish — a new objection Selina dismisses with flat denial
jokes.explanation: Selina's obsession with being reassured it's the race objection (not age) is a perfect character moment. The 'there practically isn't one' about the age difference is a great self-delusion line. Then 'He also thought you might be Jewish' adds a fresh insult that Selina deflects with the flattest possible 'Well, I'm not.' The efficiency and character specificity here are both exceptional. The joke is entirely about what Selina values — she's more offended by the implication of age or Jewishness than by being rejected for being white.

jokes.text: Dan's measured, heartfelt send-off for Jane's departure is immediately followed by 'And we'll see you bright and early Monday morning when our own Brie Ramachandran is gonna take a look at the darker side of s'mores.'
jokes.setup: Jane has just given an emotional farewell address; Dan has offered warm parting words
jokes.punchline: He immediately pivots to promoting Monday's s'mores segment with Brie
jokes.explanation: A pitch-perfect television satire button. The pivot from genuine emotion to 'the darker side of s'mores' is everything — it mocks morning show culture's ability to commodify grief and immediately move on. 'The darker side of s'mores' is also inherently funny as a news segment. Dan's delivery as the consummate TV professional makes it work. Excellent structure: the longer the farewell moment, the bigger the comedy of the pivot.

jokes.text: Jane's departure speech — 'I tried my damnedest, but this world is just... it's full of snakes and snares' — while Dan performs concerned colleague face
jokes.setup: Jane delivers an emotional farewell speech implying she's a victim of the world's cruelty
jokes.punchline: The audience knows Jane was fired for sexual harassment; her self-martyrdom speech is completely disconnected from the actual circumstances
jokes.explanation: Jane's martyrdom speech — blaming the world's 'snakes and snares' for an ouster she caused herself through harassment — is the ironic payoff to the HR storyline. The gap between her self-perception and reality is maximal. Dan's presence, forced to perform support for the person who harassed him, adds an extra layer.

jokes.text: And we'll see you bright and early Monday morning when our own Brie Ramachandran is gonna take a look at the darker side of s'mores. — Don't wanna miss that.
jokes.setup: Immediately after Jane's emotional exit, Dan pivots to the next segment
jokes.punchline: The 'darker side of s'mores' as a journalistic subject, framed as unmissable television
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of Jane's emotional self-sacrifice and Dan's immediate pivot to 's'mores journalism' is a perfect satirical beat about morning television. 'The darker side of s'mores' is an absurdly specific, funny concept. Dan's 'Don't wanna miss that' is deadpan at its best — and this is the episode's final joke, making it the landing of the Dan/Jane storyline.

tmdb_overview: Selina visits Qatar. In D.C., Jonah gets a dinner invite. Dan gets an unexpected visit from an HR rep. Catherine and Marjorie have big news.
insight: Qatar diplomatic crisis triggers 88 jokes in 45 minutes—Selina's scrambling peaks here.
